REVIEW ARTICLE
NATURE COMMUNICATIONS | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-30633-9
14
NATURE COMMUNICATIONS |  (2022) 13:3409 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-30633-9 | www.nature.com/naturecommunications

===== Leng2021_NatRevNeurol+ADneuroinflammation =====
Alzheimer disease (AD) is the most common cause of 
dementia, accounting for an estimated 60–70% of cases 
worldwide. Around 10% of people aged ≥65 years are 
thought to have AD1; this figure rises to 32% in those 
aged >85 years1, among whom the annual incidence of 
AD is estimated at 6.48%2. Patients with AD present 
with gradual loss of memory and cognitive functions 
involving the language, visuospatial and executive 
domains3. The pathological hallmarks of AD in the brain 
are amyloid-β (Aβ) plaques and abnormal tau tangles4. 
On the basis of the temporal emergence of amyloid and 
tau pathologies and evidence that Aβ overproduction 
leads to AD, the amyloid cascade hypothesis was pro-
posed, which posits that Aβ accumulation is the primary 
event leading to a cascade of effects that ultimately result 
in neuronal damage5. However, accumulating evidence 
suggests that the amyloid cascade alone cannot explain 
much of the pathogenesis of AD, indicating the involve-
ment of other pathological processes6. In particular, fol-
lowing the discovery of elevated levels of inflammatory 
markers in patients with AD as well as of AD risk genes 
associated with innate immune functions, inflammation 
has emerged as a vital player7–11.
In this Review, we discuss the pathological hallmarks 
of AD, the definition of neuroinflammation and its key 
mediators, current measures to evaluate neuroinflamma-
tion in vivo, and evidence regarding the role of microglial 
activation — the key element of neuroinflammation — 
in AD. We also consider the interrelationships between 
microglial activation and amyloid and tau pathologies 
at the microscopic and molecular levels and the effect of 
microglial activation on the pathogenesis and progression 
of AD. We propose a model to explain how microglial 
activation could influence the trajectory of AD, depend-
ing on the disease stage, microglial priming and indi-
vidual susceptibility. In light of the current evidence, we 
highlight potential strategies to treat AD by modulating 
microglial activation.
Classic hallmarks of AD
Aβ accumulation has long been considered to be a central 
and initial event in the pathogenesis of AD. Amyloid pre-
cursor protein (APP) is cleaved to generate Aβ peptides, 
which range from 38 to 43 amino acids12. Aβ monomers 
can bind to one another to form Aβ oligomers, polymers 
and, eventually, insoluble amyloid plaques. Among these 
different forms, Aβ oligomers are thought to have the 
strongest toxicity to neurons13. The well-known amyloid 
cascade hypothesis posits that elevated levels of Aβ lead 
to subsequent pathological events in AD14,15. However, 
numerous clinical trials of treatments that aimed to clear 
various forms of Aβ from the brain in people with AD 
or mild cognitive impairment (MCI) produced disap-
pointing results: although the patients’ amyloid load was 
reduced, their cognitive function did not improve16,17.
The other hallmark of AD is neurofibrillary pathology, 
which includes neuritic plaques, neurofibrillary tangles 
and neuropile threads, formed by the aggregation of tau 
Neuroinflammation and microglial 
activation in Alzheimer disease:  
where do we go from here?
Fangda Leng and Paul Edison    ✉
Abstract | Alzheimer disease (AD) is the most common form of neurodegenerative disease, 
estimated to contribute 60–70% of all cases of dementia worldwide. According to the prevailing 
amyloid cascade hypothesis, amyloid-β (Aβ) deposition in the brain is the initiating event in AD, 
although evidence is accumulating that this hypothesis is insufficient to explain many aspects of 
AD pathogenesis. The discovery of increased levels of inflammatory markers in patients with AD 
and the identification of AD risk genes associated with innate immune functions suggest that 
neuroinflammation has a prominent role in the pathogenesis of AD. In this Review, we discuss the 
interrelationships between neuroinflammation and amyloid and tau pathologies as well as the 
effect of neuroinflammation on the disease trajectory in AD. We specifically focus on microglia as 
major players in neuroinflammation and discuss the spatial and temporal variations in microglial 
phenotypes that are observed under different conditions. We also consider how these cells could 
be modulated as a therapeutic strategy for AD.
Department of Brain 
Sciences, Imperial College 
London, Hammersmith 
Hospital Campus,  
London, UK.
✉e-mail: paul.edison@
imperial.ac.uk
https://doi.org/10.1038/ 
s41582-020-00435-y
Nature ReviewS | Neurology

 volume 17 | March 2021 | 157

protein4. Under physiological conditions, modified tau 
stabilizes microtubules, regulates axonal transport and 
maintains DNA structure. The pathological modifica-
tion of tau can lead to detachment of tau from microtu-
bules, resulting in synaptic loss, neuronal dysfunction 
and tau aggregation18. Compared with amyloid depo-
sition, tau accumulation in the neocortex is believed to 
occur later but to correlate more closely with cognitive 
decline in patients with AD19–21.
Neuroinflammation and its main players
Neuroinflammation generally refers to an inflammatory 
response within the CNS that can be caused by various 
pathological insults, including infection, trauma, ischae-
mia and toxins. The process is marked by the produc-
tion of pro-inflammatory cytokines, including IL-1β, 
IL-6, IL-18 and tumour necrosis factor (TNF), chemok-
ines such as C-C motif chemokine ligand 1 (CCL1), 
CCL5 and C-X-C motif chemokine ligand 1 (CXCL1), 
small-molecule messengers, including prostaglandins 
and nitric oxide (NO), and reactive oxygen species by 
innate immune cells in the CNS22. The innate immune 
cells involved in this process are primarily microglia and 
astrocytes but capillary endothelial cells and infiltrat-
ing blood cells also contribute to neuroinflammation, 
especially when the blood–brain barrier (BBB) sustains 
biochemical or mechanical damage22,23.
The release of pro-inflammatory molecules can lead 
to synaptic dysfunction, neuronal death and inhibi-
tion of neurogenesis24. IL-1β induces synaptic loss by 
increasing prostaglandin E2 production, which leads 
to presynaptic glutamate release and postsynaptic 
N-methyl-d-aspartate (NMDA) receptor activation25, 
and TNF causes neuronal death by activating TNF recep-
tor 1 (TNFR1) and recruiting caspase 8 when the nuclear 
factor-κB (NF-κB) pathway is inhibited26. In addition, 
the complement system can be activated, promoting the 
phagocytic function of microglia, which might result in 
inappropriate pruning of synapses27. Anti-inflammatory 
cytokines, such as IL-1 receptor antagonist, IL-4, IL-10 
and IL-11, are also produced during the neuroinflam-
matory process and could be part of a sophisticated 
mechanism to prevent excessive neuroinflammation7. 
However, in the context of neurodegenerative disease, 
neuroinflammation tends to be a chronic process that 
fails to resolve by itself and is considered a vital driver of 
the disease8 (Fig. 1).
Astrocytes
Astrocytes are specialized glial cells that form the scaf-
fold of the entire CNS and can be found in a protoplas-
mic form (predominantly in grey matter) or in a fibrous 
form (predominantly in white matter)28. The processes 
of astrocytes contribute to the BBB along with endothe-
lial cells and closely enveloped synapses. Consistent 
with their anatomical niche, the functions of astrocytes 
include the regulation of cerebral blood flow, the main-
tenance of fluid and neurotransmitter homeostasis, the 
induction of synapse formation, and metabolic and 
neurotrophic support for synapses29–33. Astrocytes also 
form unique perivascular channels in the CNS, known 
as the glymphatic system, which eliminate potentially 
neurotoxic waste products, including amyloid and tau 
species34,35.
Astrocytes are acknowledged to respond to patho-
logical insults (including mechanical injury, ischaemia 
and abnormal protein aggregates) through reactive gli-
osis, which is part of the neuroinflammatory process36. 
The unified term ‘reactive astrocytes’ has been used 
to describe activated astrocytes that exhibit hyper-
trophic processes and upregulation of glial fibrillary 
acidic protein (GFAP) but diversity has been observed 
in this population. Inflammatory insult has been pro-
posed to induce the A1 astrocyte phenotype through 
the NF-κB pathway, whereas ischaemia induces the 
A2 phenotype via the signal transducers and acti-
vators of transcription 3 (STAT3) pathway, with the 
‘harmful’ A1 cells being characterized by the expres-
sion of inflammatory mediators and the ‘protective’ 
A2 cells being marked by the expression of neurotrophic 
factors37. A1 astrocytes lose their homeostatic func-
tions and are able to induce the apoptosis of neurons 
and oligodendrocytes38. However, whether astrocytes 
adopt multiple distinct activation states rather than 
conforming to this dichotomous scheme is still open to 
debate39. In addition, the phenotypes and functions of 
astrocytes might vary between different neural circuits 
and cortical areas40.
Given their numerous housekeeping functions, astro-
cytes would be expected to try to regain homeostasis in 
the initial stages of AD. Indeed, amyloid-containing 
granules have been observed in astrocytes in the vicin-
ity of amyloid plaques in human brains, suggesting an 
attempt by astrocytes to clear amyloid deposition during 
the disease process41,42. Further experiments have found 
that astrocytes migrate towards amyloid plaques and 
degrade Aβ in vitro and in vivo43. However, A1 astro-
cytes, which are toxic to the CNS, are found in abun-
dance in post-mortem brain tissue from people with AD, 
implying a detrimental role of these cells38. In an animal 
model of AD, reactive astrocytes were found to release 
excessive GABA and glutamate, leading to impaired 
memory and synaptic loss44,45. Moreover, these cells con-
tribute to dysregulated microcirculation and disruption 
of the BBB, which facilitates the accumulation of Aβ 
and progression of the disease46,47. Reactive astrocytes 
might even pave the way for the formation of the earliest 
amyloid plaques48. Of note, astrocytes work closely with 
microglia and could mediate some of the toxic effects 
of microglia in disease states38.
Key points
• Neuroinflammation has demonstrated a key role in the pathogenesis of Alzheimer 
disease (AD), the most prevalent form of dementia.
• Neuroinflammation encompasses a variety of inflammatory events in the CNS under 
pathological conditions.
• Among the innate immune cells, microglia are the primary players in neuroinflammation.
• Activated microglia exhibit diverse phenotypes and have multifaceted interactions 
with amyloid-β and tau species as well as with neuronal circuits.
• Activated microglia might have diverse influences on the progression of AD, 
depending on the stage of disease, individual susceptibility and state of microglial 
priming.
• Microglia could potentially be modulated at various points in the AD trajectory to 
either prevent or modify disease progression.
www.nature.com/nrneurol
Reviews
158 | March 2021 | volume 17	

Microglia
Development and functions. Microglia are innate 
immune cells of the myeloid lineage that reside in the 
CNS. Similarities in the morphology and cell surface 
markers of activated microglia and mononuclear phago-
cytes led to the assumption that microglia are derived 
from bone marrow myeloid progenitors and later seed 
to the CNS in a similar way to other tissue-specific 
macrophages49. However, microglial progenitors have 
now been shown to arise from primitive haemato-
poiesis early in embryonic development, which is a 
process independent from definitive haematopoiesis.

Fig. 1 | Physiological function of glial cells. a | Under physiological conditions, ramified microglia survey the brain 
microenvironment with their processes, which sense damage signals and make transient contact with neuronal network 
structures, including synapses and axons. These highly dynamic cells provide immune surveillance and trophic support to the 
CNS, remove pathogens or debris, and are involved in the maintenance of synaptic homeostasis and neuronal plasticity.  
The processes of astrocytes form the outside layer of the blood–brain barrier (BBB) and closely envelop synapses. The 
physiological roles of astrocytes include the regulation of cerebral blood flow, the maintenance of synaptic homeostasis 
and neurotrophic support. b | Under a pathological challenge, activated microglia, astrocytes and macrophages migrating 
through the damaged BBB contribute to neuroinflammation. These activated cells lose homeostatic functions, reduce their 
secretion of neurotrophic factors, and produce increased amounts of pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines, which 
could aid pathogen or toxin clearance but also lead to neuronal dysfunction and damage. Activated microglia can also 
directly eliminate synaptic structures. TNF, tumour necrosis factor.
Nature ReviewS | Neurology
Reviews
	
 volume 17 | March 2021 | 159

Primitive myeloid precursors arise from the yolk sac — 
an event that requires the expression of runt-related 
transcription factor 1 (RUNX1) and macrophage colony- 
stimulating factor 1 receptor (CSF1R)50. These mye-
loid precursors are then transported to the embryonic 
head with blood flow and later (from embryonic day 8.5 
in mice) migrate to the developing brain using spe-
cific matrix metalloproteinases50,51. Maintenance of the 
microglial population following this migration is mostly 
independent of bone marrow-produced circulating 
monocytes and relies on the self-renewal of microglia. 
A microglial depletion study showed that, similarly to 
other tissue-resident macrophages, microglial popula-
tions are replenished exclusively from brain-resident 
cells through an IL-1α-dependent pathway52. However, 
evidence has shown that circulating monocytes are capa-
ble of entering the brain parenchyma and colonizing the 
microglial niche under pathological conditions in which 
the integrity of the BBB is lost, including cerebral ischae-
mia, autoimmune encephalomyelitis and whole-body 
radiotherapy52–54. These findings raise the possibility that 
circulating monocytes can contribute to AD pathology, 
in which BBB disruption is common55.
Microglial activity is believed to synchronize with the 
development, maturation and senescence of the CNS 
throughout life via the adoption of different regulatory 
networks and has important roles in developmental syn­
aptic pruning, neuronal apoptosis, maintenance of synap­
tic plasticity and immune surveillance56,57. Defective 
synaptic pruning is considered to be associated with 
autism spectrum disorders and a compromised immune 
surveillance function seems to be closely related to neu-
rodegenerative diseases56,58. Ramified microglia have been 
observed to continuously survey the CNS microenviron-
ment with their processes, sensing damage signals, includ-
ing ATP, and mediating a microglial response towards 
the injury site59,60. In the presence of an endogenous or 
exogenous pathological insult, classes of microglial sur-
face receptors, termed damage-associated molecular pat-
terns and pathogen-associated molecular patterns, can 
recognize pathogens, cell debris or abnormal proteins 
(including Aβ species) and induce a microglial response8. 
Activated microglia internalize those pathogenic spe-
cies via pinocytosis, phagocytosis or receptor-mediated 
endocytosis and try to degrade them through various endo­
cytic pathways as well as by activating the expression of 
relevant gene modules, including chemokine receptors and 
interferons, which are major components of the neuro­
inflammatory process61–63. This process usually resolves 
once the immune stimulus is eliminated; however, micro-
glia in aged brains have functional impairments and are 
prone to sustained activation, which might contribute to 
the pathogenesis of neurodegenerative diseases64,65.
Phenotypes. Microglia have a complex role in the tra-
jectory of AD owing to their diverse phenotypes and 
variety of activation pathways. Highly ramified micro-
glia can change to an amoeboid form on pathological 
stimulation59,66. Microglia in ageing brains have reduced 
branching that reduces their area of surveillance, which 
could contribute to the impairment of homeostatic 
functions65,67–69.
Interestingly, microglial morphology in diseased 
brains also varies depending on the spatial loca-
tion and stage of the disease. For instance, amyloid 
plaque-associated microglia undergo dramatic mor-
phological and electrophysiological changes, whereas 
plaque-distant microglia showed only minor changes 
over time70. Furthermore, microglia in Braak stage V–VI 
brains have more profound morphological changes 
than do those from brains at earlier disease stages71. The 
temporal diversity of microglial morphology could be 
attributed to the intensity and duration of the patholog-
ical environment72 but could also be related to the dif-
ferential responses of microglia to different stimuli such 
as Aβ or tau aggregation73. Specifically, the emergence of 
dystrophic microglia, known as ‘dark microglia’, precedes 
the development of tau pathology74,75. Soluble hyper-
phosphorylated tau is posited to drive the phenotypic 
change in microglia, causing loss of immunosurveillance 
function and facilitating disease progression through the 
formation of neurofibrillary tangles71. These findings 
imply that phenotypic changes in microglia, including 
alterations in morphology, proteomic signatures and 
behaviour, are associated with disease progression76,77.
Traditionally, researchers have relied on morphology 
and immunochemical markers, such as CD68, HLA- 
DR and arginase 1, to infer the function of microglia78,79. 
The M1–M2 paradigm of microglial activation, with 
M1 representing a pro-inflammatory phenotype and 
M2 representing an anti-inflammatory phenotype, was 
devised to facilitate the investigation of microglia8,80. 
However, accumulating evidence challenges this simpli-
fied model and suggests that microglia do not conform 
to the M1–M2 dichotomy81. Moreover, the canonical 
immunochemical markers of microglial and macro­
phage phenotypes, such as HLA-DR and CD68, lack 
specificity for the identification of pro-inflammatory 
and anti-inflammatory phenotypes82,83. Nevertheless, 
this classification is still widely in use to convey the idea 
that microglia can be protective (M2) or detrimental 
(M1) under different circumstances.
Transcriptomic studies in mouse models of AD 
have shown that disease progression is paralleled in 
microglia by a gradual transition from a homeostatic 
state to a disease-associated state. The transition to 
disease-associated microglia (DAM) is associated 
with the downregulation of homeostatic genes and 
the upregulation of known genes associated with 
AD, including apolipoprotein E (APOE), trigger-
ing receptor expressed on myeloid cells 2 (TREM2) 
and TYRO protein tyrosine kinase-binding protein 
(TYROBP)84. Microglial activation involves a two-step 
process, with a TREM2-independent initial step and 
a TREM2-dependent second step84, highlighting the 
important role of TREM2 in the pathogenesis of neu-
rodegenerative disease85. Transcriptomic studies also 
confirmed the effects of ageing on human microglial 
phenotypes, which include the downregulation of 
genes encoding cytoskeleton-associated proteins, cell 
surface receptors, and adhesion molecules and the 
upregulation of certain genes, including IL15, CXCR4, 
VEGF4 (encoding vascular endothelial growth factor 4) 
and RUNX3 (ref.86). Moreover, temporal tracking of 
www.nature.com/nrneurol
Reviews
160 | March 2021 | volume 17	

microglial activation in a mouse model of AD identified 
distinct early and late disease stage-specific cell clusters, 
with the early response state marked by genes associa­
ted with proliferation and the late state marked by immune 
response-related genes87. Further analysis confirmed 
the heterogeneity of late response microglia identified 
by the expression of co-regulated genes, validating 
previous findings of morphologically and immunolog-
ically diverse microglia in diseased brains87. An analy-
sis published in 2018, which included new data from 
mouse models of tauopathy, revealed even more diverse 
microglial subsets than DAM, which implied a differ-
ent microglial response to amyloid and tau species88. 
These transcriptomic studies have led to the estab-
lishment of reference databases of microglial gene 
expression profiles89,90.
Notably, despite substantial overlap of microglial 
transcription profiles in healthy humans and mice, 
non-negligible differences exist between the two spe-
cies, including immune competence-related genes such 
as CD58, ERAP2, GNLY and S100A12, which are spe-
cifically upregulated in human microglia86. The same 
problem exists in disease models, with studies revealing 
RNA profiles in the brains of people with AD that are 
not replicated in animal models as well as differences 
between individuals with early-onset and late-onset 
AD76,88. Additionally, substantial differences in gene and 
protein profiles are observed between plaque-associated 
and plaque-distant microglia76. These findings call for 
cautious interpretation of results from animal models 
and bulk tissues.
Of note, considerable spatial heterogeneity of micro-
glia has been observed in the brain, not only in terms 
of density and functional markers but also with regard 
to transcriptomic profiles91,92. The spatial heterogene-
ity of microglia phenotypes and regional resilience to 
age-related changes suggest that the spatial pattern of 
neurodegenerative disease — for example, the spread 
of tau pathology according to the Braak staging system — 
might be related to local microglial phenotypes93. 
Indeed, regional diversity of microglial profiles has been 
observed in brain specimens from patients with AD94,95, 
suggesting variations in the local immune environment. 
However, whether these regional differences contribute 
to the spatial and temporal progression sequence of AD 
pathogenesis remains to be elucidated.
Advances in our understanding of phenotypic 
changes in microglia have provided insights into the 
molecular mechanisms involved in microglial activation, 
leading to attempts to modify microglial transcriptomic 
profiles through upstream regulators. However, the 
views and evidence regarding the strategies and effects 
of intervention are conflicting. For example, one study 
found that increased levels of TREM2 expression in 
mouse microglia upregulated phagocytosis-related genes 
and downregulated genes associated with immune cell 
activation in a dose-dependent manner, thereby con-
ferring a neuroprotective effect96. However, another 
study showed that TREM2 activation induced an 
APOE-dependent pathway in microglia, leading to the 
loss of homeostatic function and to the upregulation of 
inflammatory molecules, and that blocking this pathway 
restored microglial homeostatic function and protected 
neurons in various disease models97.
TREM2-knockout experiments have also produced 
conflicting outcomes. For instance, one study showed 
that TREM2 knockout in the 5×FAD mouse model of 
AD resulted in reduced numbers, supressed proliferation 
and phagocytosis of microglia, which led to the expan-
sion of amyloid plaques and neuritic damage98, whereas 
another study found that TREM2 deficiency alleviated 
amyloid and tau deposition in APP/PS1 mice99. These 
findings could be attributable to differences in animal 
models and/or disease stages at the time of observation. 
Distinct gene expression profiles in early and late disease 
stage-specific microglia could result in opposing effects 
along the disease trajectory87. These contradictory results 
emphasize the importance of cautious evaluation before 
translating these findings into applications for humans.
The emergence of single-cell transcriptomic and pro-
teomic analysis has greatly challenged our perception of 
cells in health and disease. An ongoing debate is address-
ing whether we should consider the states of microglia 
as continuous or discontinuous between two extremes, 
which is difficult to determine with a limited set of 
microscopic markers. In summary, although the exact 
role of microglial phenotypic conversion and its role in 
disease progression is still under discussion, researchers 
should be aware of the spatial and temporal diversity 
of microglia that has been uncovered by cross-sectional 
and longitudinal studies (Fig. 2).
Crosstalk between glia and neurons
Neuroinflammation studies have suggested that micro-
glia, astrocytes and neurons act in a synchronized man-
ner to promote neurodegeneration. Aβ was shown to 
activate the NF-κB pathway in astrocytes, resulting in 
the increased release of complement C3, which in turn 
acts on C3a receptors on neurons and microglia, caus-
ing neuronal dysfunction and microglial activation100,101. 
Conversely, activated microglia have been shown to 
induce A1 neurotoxic astrocytes by releasing IL-1α, C1q 
and TNF38. Cellular crosstalk between microglia and 
astrocytes could form a positive feedback loop under the 
inflammatory milieu in AD, resulting in a dysregulated 
and self-amplifying inflammatory response.
Of note, dysregulated neuronal–glial crosstalk 
has also been observed in AD. Under physiological 
conditions, neuronal–microglial crosstalk via CD200– 
CD200R and CX3CL1–CX3CR1 signalling path-
ways is believed to maintain the homeostatic profiles 
of microglia102. However, the expression of CD200, 
CD200R and CX3CR1 is reduced in the brains of people 
with AD, suggesting a loss of physiological constraints 
on microglial behaviours103,104. Given the importance 
of microglial phenotypic changes, countering dysregu-
lated cellular crosstalk might be a viable approach for 
intervention.
Evaluating neuroinflammation in vivo
To decipher the role of neuroinflammation in AD, 
various clinical and preclinical approaches have been 
adopted, including histopathological examination, neu-
roimaging and the evaluation of proteomic and other 
Nature ReviewS | Neurology
Reviews
	
 volume 17 | March 2021 | 161

biomarker signatures in blood and cerebrospinal fluid 
(CSF). Histopathology provides direct visualization of 
structures across the brain but it is primarily performed 
on post-mortem specimens as biopsies are rarely per-
formed for the diagnosis of dementia. Neuroimaging 
and fluid biomarkers have the distinct advantage of being 
minimally invasive to patients and could therefore be eas-
ily applied to clinical studies and facilitate longitudinal 
investigation of neuroinflammation in humans.
Blood and CSF biomarkers
Various studies have evaluated several cytokines, 
including IL-1β, IL-2, IL-6, IL-8, IL-10, IL-12, IL-18, 
interferon-γ (IFNγ), TNFγ and transforming growth 
factor-β (TGFβ), in blood and CSF from patients with 
AD. A pooled analysis found overall elevations in IL-1β, 
IL-6, IL-12, IL-18, TNFγ and TGFβ levels in patients 
with AD compared with healthy controls, although 
substantial discrepancies exist between the studies, 
questioning the value of these traditional inflamma-
tory markers105. Furthermore, the concentrations of 
these cytokines is influenced by systemic inflammation, 
underlining the difficulties in interpreting the results.
The most up to date research has focused on novel 
glial activation markers, including chitinase 3-like 
protein 1 (YKL40), monocyte chemotactic protein 1 
(MCP1), visinin-like protein-1 (VILIP1) and GFAP106. 
A meta-analysis confirmed that CSF levels of YKL40 and 
VILIP1 were substantially increased in AD, with a good 
effect size, and that CSF levels of MCP1 were also substan-
tially increased but with a small effect size. The change 
in CSF levels of GFAP was not significant107. YKL40 is a

Fig. 2 | Different phenotypes of microglia. The figure demonstrates the multifaceted roles of microglia in neurological 
diseases. Under certain conditions, microglia have a predominately protective role by secreting anti-inflammatory 
cytokines, performing phagocytosis and supporting network remodelling, whereas under other conditions, these cells 
contribute to neuroinflammation and cause damage to neuronal networks. Phenotypic changes in microglia underlie  
this phenomenon and a pro-inflammatory–anti-inflammatory scheme has been proposed to categorize microglial 
phenotypes. However, advances in single-cell transcriptomics have revealed much more sophisticated transcription-level 
regulation that determines microglial cell fate. Nevertheless, a working model of a pro-inflammatory–anti-inflammatory 
spectrum driven by distinct transcriptomes could shed light on how microglia could be harnessed to induce beneficial 
effects in neurodegenerative diseases. Expression heatmap reproduced with permission from Galatro et al.86.  
TGFβ, transforming growth factor β; TNF, tumour necrosis factor.
www.nature.com/nrneurol
Reviews
162 | March 2021 | volume 17	

secreted 40 kDa glycoprotein named after its first three 
N-terminal amino acids. The protein is produced pri-
marily by macrophage-lineage cells and, in the case of 
AD, by activated astrocytes and microglia108. Multicentre 
cross-sectional and longitudinal studies found that the 
concentration of YKL40 was elevated from the preclinical 
stage and correlated well with disease stage, illustrating its 
potential as a biomarker109,110.
Neuroimaging
Most neuroimaging studies have been performed using 
PET with radioactive ligands that target translocator 
protein (TSPO). This protein, previously known as the 
peripheral benzodiazepine receptor (PBR), is located 
on the mitochondrial outer membrane and is expressed 
primarily by activated glial cells in the CNS. TSPO is 
thought to be a component of the mitochondrial per-
meability transition pore and to have roles in steroid 
synthesis, regulation of mitochondrial permeability and 
modulation of the inflammatory response in glial cells111. 
Elevated tracer binding to TSPO in the brain has been 
observed by autoradiography in various CNS diseases, 
including stroke, trauma and AD112,113.
The first generation of TSPO tracers included 
11C-Ro5-4864 and 11C-PK11195 (ref.114). 11C-Ro5-
4864 showed high levels of non-specific binding, but 
11C-PK11195 proved to have acceptable binding spec-
ificity and became the most extensively applied tracer 
in neuroinflammation imaging studies. A second gen-
eration of TSPO tracers was developed with improved 
imaging characteristics, including higher affinity 
and lower lipophilicity, which resulted in a higher 
signal-to-noise ratio. The second-generation tracers 
include 11C-PBR28, 11C-DAA1106, 11C-DPA713 and 
18F-PBR06, whereas 18F-GE180 and 11C-ER176 are being 
tested as third-generation tracers115.
The binding affinity of second-generation trac-
ers to TSPO is influenced by the co-dominant rs6971 
(Ala147Thr) single-nucleotide polymorphism in the TSPO 
gene. Ala/Ala carriers are high-affinity binders (HAB), 
whereas Thr/Thr carriers are low-affinity binders (LAB) 
and Ala/Thr carriers are mixed-affinity binders (MAB). 
The affinity difference between HAB and LAB ranges from 
approximately fourfold for 11C-DAA1106, 11C-DPA713 
and 11C-PBR111 to approximately 50-fold for 11C-PBR28 
(ref.116). Thus, imaging studies might need to account for 
TSPO genotypes, as mixing HAB and MAB populations 
could confound the findings117. Fortunately, the TSPO 
genotype does not seem to influence AD prevalence, amy-
loid loads or rates of cognitive decline, which are similar 
in HAB, MAB and LAB cohorts, suggesting that findings 
from HAB and MAB populations could be applied to the 
entire AD cohort118.
TSPO tracers are not able to distinguish the contri-
butions of different cells or glial phenotypes to the neu-
roinflammatory response. In particular, the extent to 
which astrocytes contribute to the TSPO tracer binding 
signal in vivo is under ongoing debate. The selective acti-
vation of astrocytes induced by lentiviral gene transfer 
was shown to lead to TSPO overexpression that could be 
detected by PET in rats119. TSPO tracer binding to reac-
tive astrocytes has also been reported in various disease 
models, including AD and ischaemia120,121. However, 
a post-mortem study on human brains found correla-
tions between the binding of tracers (11C-PK11195 and 
11C-DAA1106) and activated microglia but not between 
binding of these tracers and reactive astrocytes in var-
ious CNS diseases122. The expression of the TSPO gene 
seems to vary across different phenotypes of activated 
microglia depending on the stimulus, with lipopolysac-
charide being a strong inducer and neurodegeneration 
providing only a mild stimulus88. Even in the context of 
AD, Aβ deposition and hyperphosphorylated tau might 
induce different patterns of TSPO expression120.
Efforts have been made to find novel targets for 
PET tracers, including intracellular enzymes or mol-
ecules such as cyclooxygenases 1 and 2, monoamine 
oxidase B, glycogen synthase kinase 3, arachidonic acid, 
and receptors such as the purinergic receptors (P2X7 and 
P2Y12), CX3CR1, cannabinoid receptor 2 and TREM1 
(refs123,124). Novel tracers targeting these molecules might 
provide additional information about different glial cell 
phenotypes125.
Interactions of Aβ, tau and microglia
Reactive microglia have been established to closely 
colocalize with amyloid plaques in the brains of people 
with AD, indicating an interaction between this impor-
tant pathological hallmark of AD and microglia126,127. 
Associations between pathological tau load and 
microglial cell load have also been described in both 
post-mortem and in vivo neuroimaging studies128,129. 
The sequential emergence of amyloid plaques, micro-
glial activation and neurofibrillary tangles led to the 
amyloid cascade–inflammation hypothesis, which sug-
gests that microglial activation acts as a bridge leading 
to the pathological phosphorylation and aggregation of 
tau128,130,131. The complex interactions between the three 
hallmarks of AD have become an interesting topic of 
research.
Early studies established that different species of Aβ 
aggregates could induce glial activation and the produc-
tion of pro-inflammatory cytokines (including IL-1β, 
IL-6, IL-8 and TNF), the anti-inflammatory cytokine 
TGFβ, chemokines (including MCP1 and macrophage 
inflammatory protein 1), various cell adhesion mol-
ecules, NO, and reactive oxygen species, all of which 
could lead to neuronal dysfunction and death132–134. 
Among all Aβ species, the smaller oligomers of Aβ 
have been found to be far stronger stimuli to induce the 
microglial response and to be more toxic to neurons 
than larger oligomers135.
The receptors by which microglia recognize Aβ spe-
cies are mostly pattern recognition receptors, including 
Toll-like receptors (for example, TLR1, TLR2, TLR4 and 
TLR6), CD14, CD47, α6β1 integrin and scavenger recep-
tors (including CD36). In turn, these receptors activate 
molecular pathways that induce microglial phenotypic 
changes136–140. For instance, the binding of Aβ fibrils to 
TLR2 induces IL-8 and TNF expression137, the activa-
tion of scavenger receptors in the presence of Aβ upreg-
ulates IL-1β and NO production through NF-κB, c-Jun 
N-terminal kinase (JNK) and mitogen-activated protein 
kinase (MAPK) pathways138, and the recognition of Aβ 
Nature ReviewS | Neurology
Reviews
	
 volume 17 | March 2021 | 163

by the CD36–CD47–α6β1 integrin complex mediates 
phagocytic changes in microglia141.
One molecule that is of specific interest in the 
Aβ-induced microglial inflammatory response is 
the transient receptor potential melastatin-related 2 
(TRPM2) channel — a calcium-permeable chan-
nel induced by oxidative stress142. Aβ42 is proposed to 
induce oxidative stress through the activation of pro-
tein kinase C and NADPH oxidase, which in turn acti-
vate poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase 1, ADP-ribose and 
TRPM2. The resulting calcium influx then activates 
cytokine production and the proline-rich tyrosine kinase 
2–MAPK positive feedback pathway139. The recruitment 
of the pyrin domain-containing 3 (NLRP3) inflam-
masome has also been revealed to be a crucial event 
in the pro-inflammatory response of microglia and is 
followed by the expression of caspase 1 and maturation 
of IL-1β143,144. The recruitment of NLRP3 is a two-step 
process, with NF-κB-induced upregulation of NLRP3 as 
the initial step and a further stimulus causing a confor-
mational change in NLRP3 that leads to inflammasome 
assembly145. Although the signals mediating this second 
step are yet to be elucidated, one study suggested that 
TRPM2-mediated calcium influx was capable of induc-
ing NLRP3 assembly139. Other receptors, including com-
plement receptors and Fc receptors on microglia, also 
regulate neuroinflammation by communicating with 
the innate and adaptive immune system without directly 
interacting with Aβ, thereby mediating phagocytosis as 
well as a pro-inflammatory response146.
Another microglial receptor that is of particular inter-
est is TREM2, the Arg47 and His62 variants of which are 
genetic risk factors for AD10,147. The enhanced expression 
of TREM2 has been found on plaque-associated micro-
glia in patients with AD as well as in a mouse model 
of AD99. TREM2 signalling has been suggested to pro-
mote microglial proliferation, phagocytosis and cytokine 
secretion and to regulate microglial metabolism and 
survival85,148. TREM2 is a tempting target for pharmaco-
logical modulation but caution should be exercised as 
TREM2 is a vital upstream mediator of microglial acti-
vation and phenotypic change84,97. Changes in TREM2 
function might have an effect on both beneficial and 
detrimental microglial functions in AD.
The phagocytic function of activated microglia is 
well established, with evidence of internalization of Aβ 
aggregates by cultured microglia. However, the degra-
dation of Aβ species after phagocytosis is fairly slow, 
which raises questions regarding the effectiveness of 
microglia in Aβ clearance149. Autophagy is suggested to 
be one of the vital processes involved in the degrada-
tion of Aβ fibrils150,151. An in vitro study indicated dif-
ferential responses of microglia to different Aβ species 
as these cells were shown to phagocytose fibrils but not 
oligomers, with the latter species preferentially eliciting 
the production of inflammatory cytokines and recip-
rocally suppressing the phagocytosis of fibrillary Aβ152. 
However, whether microglia can efficiently regulate 
Aβ levels in vivo is unclear153,154 as pathological examina-
tions of brain tissue from people with late-stage AD have 
found microglia in a morphologically dystrophic state, 
with decreased branch lengths and areas of coverage 
as well as the fragmentation of processes suggesting a 
loss of surveillance function72,75. Further studies have 
also found limited amounts of microglia expressing 
TREM2 and CD68 in the vicinity of amyloid plaques 
in people with Down syndrome or AD, suggesting that 
plaque-associated microglia have a compromised phago-
cytic function155. Interestingly, such dystrophic change 
is also associated with ageing, the default risk factor for 
AD156, implying a role for dysfunctional microglia in the 
development of AD pathology157. In addition, evidence 
indicates that the cytokines produced by microglia, 
including IL-1β, IL-6, TNF and IFNγ, can upregulate 
the production of β-secretase, an enzyme that cleaves 
APP to generate pathogenic Aβ, putatively through the 
NF-κB pathway158,159. However, an in vivo study demon-
strated that microglia can limit the diffusion and con-
densation of amyloid plaques and that the inhibition of 
these cells increased the toxicity of plaques98.
Intriguingly, microglia might not only indirectly 
facilitate the production of Aβ but may also promote the 
seeding of amyloid plaques. An animal study reported 
that apoptosis-associated speck-like protein containing 
a C-terminal caspase recruitment domain (ASC), the 
adaptor protein in NLRP3 inflammasome activation, 
forms particles that serve as a binding core for amy-
loid aggregation and that these particles can spread to 
other myeloid cells160. Considering the toxicity of sol-
uble amyloid species, ASC might have a beneficial role 
by facilitating the deposition of amyloid, although the 
neuroprotective effect of ASC knockout makes this 
scenario unlikely160 (Fig. 3).
In vitro studies have found that tau oligomers and 
fibrils provide sufficient stimulus to induce microglial 
morphological change and expression of interleukins161. 
Further investigation using mouse models of tauop-
athy validated the existence of tau-induced micro-
glial activation in vivo, marked by the upregulation 
of DAM-associated genes, including phagocytic and 
inflammatory modules, with CD68 and TYROBP serv-
ing as major hubs162. The upregulation of phagocytic 
genes is supported by the observation that tau fibrils 
are internalized by microglia both in cell culture and 
in healthy mouse brains163. The activation of the com-
plement system by tau fibrils through classical and 
alternative pathways is suggested to be a vital event in 
tau-mediated microglial activation162. However, evi-
dence from pathological examination of human brains 
has found that microglia associated with tau pathology 
show morphological degeneration, suggesting impaired 
function71,75. These dystrophic changes are proposed to 
result from microglial senescence or chronic amyloid 
poisoning and to chronologically precede the spread 
of tau pathology164. Moreover, analogous to their inter-
action with Aβ, microglia seem to actively spread tau 
through phagocytosis and exosome secretion, provid-
ing an intraneuronal propagation pathway in addition 
to trans-synaptic transmission of tau165, thereby con-
tributing to disease progression. More intriguingly, in 
the context of AD pathology, Aβ can exacerbate tau 
pathology in synergy with microglia166. NLRP3 activa-
tion in microglia is a vital event bridging the two core 
AD pathologies167 and the increased tau load seems to 
www.nature.com/nrneurol
Reviews
164 | March 2021 | volume 17	

facilitate the microglial clearance of Aβ by promoting 
phagocytosis168.
The complexity of the interactions described above 
is probably attributable to different responses of micro-
glia towards different stimuli. For example, a gene 
co-expression network analysis in Drosophila mod-
els found that the TREM2–TYROBP pathway, which 
is predominantly a microglial signalling pathway, 
interacts with both Aβ42 and tau-induced pathways 
at the gene expression level169. Furthermore, over half 
of the pathways induced by Aβ–TREM2–TYROBP 
co-expression coincided with those induced by tau–
TREM2–TYROBP, suggesting that microglia act as 
a common mediator or bridging factor for tau and 
amyloid toxicities. Interestingly, the study found that 
TREM signalling could suppress half of the pathways 
induced by Aβ, implying a defensive role for microglia. 
However, in a tau–TREM2–TYROBP co-expression 
model, microglia acted synergistically to exacerbate 
tau-mediated neurodegeneration, highlighting the dif-
ferent microglial responses to Aβ and tau. In support 
of these findings, a study published in 2017 observed 
that progranulin deficiency led to increased numbers of 
CD68+ microglia and prevented amyloid plaque growth 
while exacerbating neuronal damage and microglia 
tau pathology, which is thought to be mediated by the 
microglial TREM2–TYROBP network170. Similarly, neu-
ronal CX3CL1 or microglial CX3CR membrane protein 
deficiency was reported to have opposing effects on 
amyloid and tau formation via neuronal–microglial 
interactions171,172.
These observations provide a possible explanation for 
the phenomenon, described repeatedly in clinical stud-
ies, that microglial activation seems to protect patients 
with MCI from deterioration but has an opposite role 
at the AD stage when tau pathology predominates173,174. 
Indeed, people with MCI showed a weak correlation 
between tau deposition and microglial activation129,175, 
which became stronger upon progression to AD129.
Neuroinflammation and AD trajectory
Neuroinflammation might be a vital component in 
the initiation of AD pathology. Epidemiological stud-
ies have shown positive associations between demen-
tia and previous reports of infection, smoking and 
diabetes176. A potential protective role of non-steroidal 
anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) against AD has 
also been proposed177,178, although the efficacy of these 
drugs is still debated. Systemic inflammation induced 
by the aforementioned risk factors can induce CNS neu-
roinflammation through circumventricular organs, the 
vagus nerve, active transport or disruption of the BBB7. 
Post-mortem studies in young patients who had experi-
enced systemic inflammation found morphological and 
immunological changes in microglia that were similar 
to those observed in aged individuals or people with 
dementia74. Traumatic brain injury, which is suspected 
to increase the risk of late-life dementia, has also been 
found to induce persistent neuroinflammation179.
Microglial activation has been observed at the 
pre-plaque stage in animal models of AD180 and a 
human neuroimaging study reported increased micro-
glial activation in people with MCI in the absence of 
amyloid tracer uptake181–186. Both of these findings 
suggest that neuroinflammation is an early event in 
AD. Further studies found that the injection of Aβ 
alone into the brain was insufficient to induce amyloid 
pathology in primate models but that the co-injection 
of lipopolysaccharide and Aβ or that of Aβ into ani-
mal models of chronic systemic inflammation could 
cause amyloid plaque formation187. More-compelling 
evidence came from a human post-mortem study, 
which found no relevant morphological changes in 
microglia in asymptomatic individuals with elevated 
amyloid loads at various ages75, strongly suggesting that 
neuroinflammation, in addition to Aβ deposition, is 
a prerequisite for AD pathogenesis. An initiating role 
for neuroinflammation in AD is also supported by evi-
dence from genome-wide association studies, which 
found that mutations of microglial or innate immune 
genes, such as CD33, TREM2 and complement recep-
tor type 1, are associated with an increased occurrence 
of AD in the population10,188,189. The accumulating evi-
dence suggests that microglial priming occurs in the 
context of AD, referring to a process whereby chronic 
low-level stimuli (including systemic inflammation and 
ageing) cause naive microglia to adopt an altered state 
that results in an exaggerated or inappropriate inflam-
matory response when these microglia are exposed to 
repeated pathological stimuli190.

Fig. 3 | Microglial response to amyloid-β species. Amyloid-β (Aβ) species can be 
recognized by a range of pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) on microglia, which further 
activate phagocytic or inflammatory pathways. The upregulation of their phagocytic 
capacity enables microglia to internalize and degrade Aβ and activation of the nuclear 
factor-κB (NF-κB) pathway leads to the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines and 
assembly of the pyrin domain-containing 3 (NLRP3) inflammasome. NLRP3 inflammasome 
activation can lead to the release of apoptosis-associated speck-like protein containing a 
C-terminal caspase recruitment domain (ASC) specks, which might serve as binding cores 
for Aβ aggregation. The release of pro-inflammatory cytokines could promote further 
glial activation, neuronal damage and upregulation of β-secretase (BACE), an enzyme 
that is involved in the production of pathogenic Aβ species. TNF, tumour necrosis factor.
Nature ReviewS | Neurology
Reviews
	
 volume 17 | March 2021 | 165

The influence of neuroinflammation on the symp-
tomatic stage of AD has been demonstrated in clinical 
imaging studies that reported negative correlations 
between microglial activation and structural integrity 
or functional activity of the brain in patients with AD as 
evaluated by hippocampal volume and glucose metab-
olism, respectively191,192. Furthermore, imaging studies 
evaluating brain connectivity in people with AD implied 
an association between microglial activation and dis-
ruption of intrinsic functional connectivity193,194 as well 
as white matter microstructural breakdown195. From a 
clinical perspective, an inverse relationship has been 
reported between cognitive performance and microglial 
activation but not with brain amyloid load in individuals 
with AD196–198.
The clinical imaging observations are supported by 
preclinical studies, which found that microglia activated 
by Aβ could produce neurotoxic cytokines and chemok-
ines, including TNF, IL-6, IL-1β and CCL2, which sub-
sequently cause neuronal dysfunction and death154,199–201. 
Moreover, microglia activated through the comple-
ment pathway can inappropriately engulf synapses, 
leading to early synaptic loss and defects in long-term 
potentiation27,202. Consistent with this finding, the knock-
out of complement C3 protected against synaptic degener-
ation in the hippocampus in APP/PS1 mice, which showed 
elevated Aβ deposition but reduced levels of ionized cal-
cium binding adaptor molecule 1 (IBA1), CD68 immuno-
reactivity and pro-inflammatory cytokine production203. 
In accordance with the neuroimaging findings, a study in 
people with AD reported that amyloid plaques exerted 
increasing local toxicity as the disease progressed204. The 
researchers found no significant increase in brain amyloid 
load throughout the clinical stage but discovered increased 
CD68+ microglial activation, suggesting that microglia 
mediate amyloid toxicity. The circuit-specific preference 
of abnormal synaptic pruning might also contribute to the 
phenomenon whereby neurodegeneration and tau distri-
bution follows certain predictable patterns that correlate 
poorly with Aβ deposition205,206.
These promising findings have led to further studies 
on the dynamic changes in neuroinflammation from the 
MCI stage of AD to the late dementia stage. In a mixed 
AD and MCI cohort, a progressive increase in 11C-PBR28 
uptake correlated with the annual rate of cognitive 
decline186. Another longitudinal study that investigated 
the dynamics of microglial activation with 11C-PK11195 
specifically in an AD cohort found an increase in micro-
glial activation in six of eight patients over a 16-month 
period, with adynamic changes in microglial location 
and an accompanying metabolic decline207. Interestingly, 
two patients showed a longitudinal decline in micro-
glial activation207, which could reflect the ‘burnout’ of 
microglia. This hypothesis is supported by the patho-
logical finding of decreased IBA1-positive microglia in a 
subgroup of Braak stage V–IV brains71.
In a cohort of patients with AD, including prodromal 
and dementia stages (Clinical Dementia Rating 0.5 and 
≥1, respectively), higher baseline binding of a microglial 
tracer in the brain was associated with a slower rate of 
disease progression over a 2-year follow-up period, with 
individuals showing fast cognitive decline tending to 
have a lower baseline binding173. This finding suggested 
a protective role of neuroinflammation in the early or 
even preclinical stages of AD, which could be explained 
by the capacity of microglia to degrade and clear Aβ154. 
In a subsequent follow-up study, the same cohort of 
patients underwent a second set of 18F-DPA-714 scans208. 
Comparable increases in microglial activation occurred 
in the prodromal and dementia groups and two dis-
tinctive dynamic patterns of microglial activation were 
observed: a higher initial binding followed by a slower 
increase and a lower initial binding followed by a more 
rapid increase. The former pattern was associated with 
an improved outcome whereas the latter suggested 
accelerated disease progression208. Intriguingly, another 
longitudinal study found distinct patterns of microglial 
activation in people with MCI or AD: the MCI group 
showed decreasing levels of 11C-PK11195 uptake over 
time, whereas the AD group showed a longitudinal 
increase, implying two peaks of microglial activation174.
The discrepant findings of these two longitudinal 
studies174,208 could be partly attributed to different inclu-
sion criteria; the first cohort could represent a narrow 
time span of disease trajectory with strict inclusion and 
exclusion criteria, which could explain why no clear dif-
ference in 18F-DPA-714 binding was seen between the 
prodromal and dementia groups despite the longitudi-
nal increase of 18F-DPA-714 and 11C-PK11195 binding 
in both groups208. The distinct patterns of microglial 
activation observed in these two studies could reflect 
individual variation in the susceptibility to pathological 
processes and neuroinflammation-mediated damage or 
the consequence of prior microglial priming.
Some of the variations in the PET imaging studies 
using different tracers (or even the same tracer) could 
be attributable to differing methodologies but might also 
depend on the extent to which microglial activation can 
be detected in people with MCI or whether microglial 
activation plateaus or fluctuates in the later stages of 
AD209–211. Sample size or tracer properties could account 
for the discrepancies but, more importantly, the methods 
of tracer analysis and other strategies of image analysis 
both have a considerable influence on the results129,212–214.
A meta-analysis of the evidence from various imag-
ing studies has provided helpful insights into the spatial 
and temporal evolution of neuroinflammation in AD215. 
The authors reported more profound neuroinflam-
mation in AD than in MCI, in line with a longitudinal 
increase in microglial activation along the AD trajectory. 
Furthermore, an inverse association between parietal 
neuroinflammation and Mini-Mental State Examination 
scores was found in patients with AD, supporting a detri-
mental role of microglial activation in the later stages of 
the disease. The study also revealed the spatial pattern 
of neuroinflammation in the disease process, with micro-
glial activation starting in the neocortex at the MCI stage 
and spreading ventrally throughout the brain, including 
the cerebellum, with an emphasis on temporal regions 
where tau pathology dominates in the late stage of AD215.
Current evidence generally supports a model of 
neuroinflammation in which the chronic background 
inflammation that occurs with ageing provides an initial 
mild stimulus that results in microglial priming, which 
www.nature.com/nrneurol
Reviews
166 | March 2021 | volume 17	

is followed by a possible protective wave of microglial 
activation in the preclinical stage of AD, along with the 
emergence of Aβ deposition. Ineffective clearance of Aβ 
combined with tau aggregation impair microglial defence 
functions and elicit an ongoing detrimental microglial 
activation process in late-stage AD. The effectiveness and 
persistence of the initial response is influenced by individ-
ual susceptibility and prior baseline inflammation, which 
could prime microglia towards phenotypes that have an 
impaired defence potential and an increased tendency to 
transform to the late-stage harmful state. This process is 
probably driven by phenotypic changes in microglia in 
response to the microenvironment and toxic stimuli (Fig. 4).
Therapeutic implications
Understanding the role of microglia along the AD tra-
jectory enables us to intervene in three different ways: 
first, by suppressing the pro-inflammatory properties 
of microglia to limit the deleterious effect of microglial 
activation; second, by modulating microglial pheno-
typic change to favour anti-inflammatory properties; 
and third, by influencing microglial priming early in 
the disease process.
Suppressing pro-inflammatory properties
Suppression of neuroinflammation with NSAIDs was 
among the earliest approaches to AD treatment; however, 
the general use of NSAIDs in symptomatic individuals 
with AD was found to be ineffective in several clinical 
trials216. One possibility is that non-specific suppression 
of both the pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory 
phenotypes is not an effective strategy. Another com-
pound of interest is minocycline, a widely used tetra-
cycline antibiotic believed to have anti-inflammatory 
effects through the inhibition of inducible NO synthase, 
IL-1β converting enzyme, and matrix metalloprotein-
ases and by suppression of microglial activation217. 
Animal studies showed that minocycline could reverse 
the upregulation of IL-1β, IL-10 and TNF as well as the 
memory impairment caused by Aβ218. However, in a 
clinical trial published in 2019, minocycline at 200 mg 
or 400 mg per day did not preserve cognition in patients 
with mild AD over a 2-year period219.
These results highlight the need for compounds 
that specifically target pro-inflammatory phenotypes. 
Attempts have been made to target the NF-κB, NLPR3– 
caspase 1 and p38MAPK pathways, thereby suppress-
ing the pro-inflammatory microglial phenotype220–222. 
The caspase 1 inhibitor VX-765 showed some potential 
to suppress microglial activation, Aβ deposition and 
neuro­degeneration, and to alleviate cognitive decline in 
a mouse model of AD223, suggesting that this more precise 
approach is more effective than counteracting neuroin-
flammation in general. However, compounds for clinical 
application are still under development and their efficacy 
requires testing in clinical trials.
An even more specific approach targets downstream 
pro-inflammatory cytokines. Preclinical trials showed 
that an anti-TNF antibody could effectively alleviate 
amyloid pathology and tau phosphorylation224,225. A clin-
ical pilot study also showed the potential of etanercept, 
a TNF blocker, to improve language performance in a 
small cohort of patients with AD226. A further phase II 
clinical trial has reported good tolerance of etanercept in 
people with AD, although its therapeutic effects remain 
to be validated in phase III trials227. Intraperitoneal 
administration of an IL-1 receptor-blocking antibody 
has also shown some potential to improve cognition 
and reduce tau pathology in mouse models of AD, but 
further clinical trials are needed to evaluate the safety 
and efficacy of this antibody in humans228.
Modulating microglial phenotypic change
In the very early stages of the AD trajectory, microglia are 
known to maintain neuronal function by clearing toxic 
damage. Microglial activation can be interfered with in 
such a way that these anti-inflammatory properties can 
be maintained for prolonged periods. Several groups 
are developing strategies to modulate microglial activa-
tion towards an anti-inflammatory and/or phagocytic 
phenotype.

Fig. 4 | Dynamic changes in microglial activation affect Alzheimer disease 
progression. This figure explains why patients with defective microglial functioning  
(for example, due to acquired or genetic susceptibility) might develop cognitive decline at 
an earlier pathological stage of Alzheimer disease (AD), as defined by dynamic changes 
in their amyloid and tau loads249, than do those with normal microglial function. Current 
evidence suggests that, at the early, asymptomatic, stage of AD, the initial microglial 
response is protective overall. However, as AD progresses, chronic activation causes 
phenotypic changes in microglia and shifts their behaviour towards a pro-inflammatory 
phenotype, which causes damage to neuronal networks. Inappropriate microglial 
responses can occur in individuals with defective protective functions owing to genetic 
or acquired factors such as microglial priming. Microglia in those individuals might  
have a weak initial protective response, a quicker transition to phenotypes that are 
detrimental overall and an exaggerated late-stage deleterious inflammatory response. 
Individual susceptibility to Alzheimer disease (in terms of more profound clinical 
presentation at a given pathological stage) might therefore be partly determined by 
microglial behaviour. MCI, mild cognitive impairment.
Nature ReviewS | Neurology
Reviews
	
 volume 17 | March 2021 | 167

A preliminary clinical study has investigated whether 
subcutaneous administration of IFNβ1a can modulate 
neuroinflammation in patients with early AD. This treat-
ment was well tolerated and the results showed a trend 
towards slowing of disease progression229. The sirtuin 1 
agonist resveratrol, which activates caloric restriction 
mechanisms, markedly reduced CSF levels of inflamma-
tory molecules, including matrix metallopeptidase 9 and 
IL-4, and showed considerable potential to attenuate cog-
nitive decline in early AD230. These results should encour-
age further clinical study of these medications, in addition 
to approaches aimed at modulating microglial activation.
Various other anti-inflammatory cytokines, includ-
ing IL-2, IL-4 and IL-33, have shown the potential to 
modulate microglial activation and alleviate AD pathol-
ogy in preclinical studies231–233. Although the results are 
not conclusive234, these candidate agents merit further 
exploration, including through evaluation in large-scale 
preclinical studies. The direct delivery of these cytokines 
into humans remains a challenge as most preclini-
cal studies have used viral vectors as a gene expres-
sion system234 and novel strategies to modulate these 
cytokines might be required.
Extensive efforts have been made to modulate other 
targets, including nuclear receptors such as peroxisome 
proliferator-activated receptor-γ (PPARγ)235. The PPARγ 
agonists pioglitazone and rosiglitazone are able to shift 
microglia from a pro-inflammatory to a phagocytic 
phenotype and facilitate amyloid clearance in animal 
models236,237. However, rosiglitazone failed to show 
substantial efficacy in phase III clinical trials, probably 
owing to its poor BBB permeation and safety concerns238. 
In another study, the inhibition of the NLRP3 inflam-
masome showed protective effects against amyloid 
pathology and, strikingly, against amyloid-induced 
tau hyperphosphorylation and aggregation in animal 
models167,239. However, the efficacy of this approach in 
humans remains to be evaluated.
Influencing microglial priming
Microglial priming is associated not only with age-
ing but also with systemic inflammation in various 
animal models and primed microglia show enhanced 
sensitivity and reactivity to inflammatory stimuli240. 
Thus, a possible therapeutic approach in AD is to sup-
press microglial priming during the pre-disease period. 
In people with AD, midlife obesity, insulin resistance 
and elevated LDL levels have been suggested to induce 
an inflammatory milieu via endothelial cells and statins 
showed protective effects against this mechanism241,242. 
Clinical trials have also suggested a role for dietary 
intervention in the management of neuroinflammation 
as folic acid and omega 3 fatty acid supplementation led 
to a reduction in the levels of inflammatory markers in 
CSF and plasma in people with AD243,244. Therefore, a 
multifaceted approach to counteract microglial prim-
ing might be beneficial. However, a large-scale 3-year 
clinical trial showed no significant long-term effects of 
omega 3 fatty acid supplementation plus an interven-
tion comprising physical activity, cognitive training and 
nutritional advice245. Positive results were reported from 
the Finnish Geriatric Intervention Study to Prevent 
Cognitive Impairment and Disability (FINGER) cohort, 
in which an intervention including diet, exercise and 
vascular risk management helped to maintain cognitive 
function in a population at risk of dementia246. However, 
the complex actions of multidomain interventions in 
real-life settings are still being interrogated in ongoing 
clinical trials247,248.
Conclusions
Current evidence suggests that neuroinflammation has 
a vital role in the pathogenesis and progression of AD. 
In this Review, we have proposed that differential micro-
glial responses to pathological stimuli have a substantial 
influence on AD progression, that this dynamic process 
has variable effects along the disease trajectory, and 
that the microglial response depends on individual sus-
ceptibility and could be influenced by prior microglial 
priming. Interventions to target microglial priming in 
the pre-disease period and to modulate the microglial 
response in the brain once the disease process is under-
way are showing promise as part of a disease-modifying 
strategy.

===== Leung2019_DevDyn_SpinalCordPatterning+Evolution =====
R E V I E W
Evolution of vertebrate spinal cord patterning
Brigid Leung
|
Sebastian M. Shimeld
Department of Zoology, University of
Oxford, Oxford, UK
Correspondence
Sebastian M. Shimeld, Department of
Zoology, University of Oxford, Zoology
Research and Administration Building, 11a
Mansfield Road, Oxford OX1 3SZ, UK.
Email: sebastian.shimeld@zoo.ox.ac.uk
Funding information
Elizabeth Hannah Jenkinson Fund; John Fell
Fund; BBSRC
Abstract
The vertebrate spinal cord is organized across three developmental axes, anterior-
posterior (AP), dorsal-ventral (DV), and medial-lateral (ML). Patterning of these
axes is regulated by canonical intercellular signaling pathways: the AP axis by
Wnt, fibroblast growth factor, and retinoic acid (RA), the DV axis by Hedgehog,
Tgfβ, and Wnt, and the ML axis where proliferation is controlled by Notch. Devel-
opmental time plays an important role in which signal does what and when. Pat-
terning across the three axes is not independent, but linked by interactions between
signaling pathway components and their transcriptional targets. Combined this
builds a sophisticated organ with many different types of cell in specific AP, DV,
and ML positions. Two living lineages share phylum Chordata with vertebrates,
amphioxus, and tunicates, while the jawless fish such as lampreys, survive as the
most basally divergent vertebrate lineage. Genes and mechanisms shared between
lampreys and other vertebrates tell us what predated vertebrates, while those also
shared with other chordates tell us what evolved early in chordate evolution.
Between these lie vertebrate innovations: genetic and developmental changes
linked to evolution of new morphology. These include gene duplications, differ-
ences in how signals are received, and new regulatory connections between signal-
ing pathways and their target genes.
K E Y W O R D S
amphioxus, BMP, delta, FGF, lamprey, morphogen, neural, Notch, RA, Shh, Wnt
1
| THE EVOLUTION OF THE
VERTEBRATE NERVOUS SYSTEM
Vertebrates develop complex nervous systems with a level of
sophistication that far exceeds that of invertebrates. The only
challengers to this claim might be cephalopod molluscs, and
some of the larger arthropods. Many of the complexities shown
by vertebrates are considered innovations,1,2 and evolutionary
biologists have long been interested in when and how verte-
brate neural complexity evolved. Most studies on this topic
have focused on the brain or the neural crest. This is not sur-
prising, since the brain is the center of characteristics such as
upper level neural processing and memory, while the neural
crest has long been recognized as a pivotally important tissue
in vertebrate evolution, giving rise to many structures consid-
ered to be vertebrate innovations.3,4 There is, however, more to
the vertebrate nervous system than the brain and neural crest.
Nervous systems are typically divided into central (CNS) and
peripheral (PNS) components. The vertebrate PNS derives pri-
marily form neural crest cells, with cranial neurogenic placodes
also playing a role. The evolution of neural crest and placodes
has been extensively reviewed elsewhere5-7 and will not be fur-
ther considered here. The CNS is often dichotomized into the
brain and spinal cord. While this makes sense from anatomical
and functional perspectives, whether the separation is justified
on developmental and evolutionary grounds is much more
debatable. Aspects of spinal cord organization and develop-
ment extend into the hindbrain, including patterning by Hox
Received: 29 April 2019
Revised: 14 June 2019
Accepted: 15 June 2019
DOI: 10.1002/dvdy.77
1028
© 2019 Wiley Periodicals, Inc.
Developmental Dynamics. 2019;248:1028–1043.
wileyonlinelibrary.com/journal/dvdy

genes, suggesting the separation may be better thought of as
into a posterior, Hox-expressing territory (hindbrain and spinal
cord) and anterior Hox-free territory (midbrain and forebrain).
Recent work also shows deep and fundamental differences
may exist in the gene regulatory networks (GRNs) that regulate
the formation of the anterior and posterior neural cells very
early in development,8 and some authors have even speculated
there may be separate evolutionary origins for anterior and pos-
terior CNS components, traceable to separate anterior and pos-
terior nervous systems in an early ancestor of the majority of
extant animals.9
There is clearly still much to learn about the similarities
and differences between anterior and posterior CNS in verte-
brates and other animal groups, but regardless of the out-
come, the spinal cord has received relatively little attention
from evolutionary biologists. This is in stark contrast to
developmental biologists, who have dissected spinal cord
development with impressive detail. This reflects a combina-
tion of its relative simplicity and its experimental tractability
for gene manipulation and imaging. It has resulted in the spi-
nal cord becoming something of a “model organ system” for
understanding core developmental processes such as mor-
phogen patterning and the balance between proliferation and
differentiation.10 This depth of knowledge represents an
opportunity to explore the relationship between genetic
change at the gene and genome level, developmental pro-
cesses, and phenotypic consequences in terms of nervous
system complexity. Key phenotypic changes in this regard
might include increased cell number, new cell types, innova-
tion in cell patterning, and mechanisms for axon navigation.
In this review, we will first summarize how vertebrate spinal
cords develop. We will then relate this to genetic change,
including gene duplication, with comparison to early diverg-
ing vertebrates such as lampreys, and to the vertebrates'
nearest invertebrate relatives, tunicates, and amphioxus
(Figure 1).
2
| SPINAL CORD
DEVELOPMENTAL ANATOMY
The adult spinal cord of vertebrates serves a number of func-
tions. Most obviously, it relays signals between the periph-
ery and the brain. Put simplistically, sensory information is
received via the dorsal nerve roots and passed on to the
brain, while motor instructions are passed from the brain to
the motor neurons which connect to the muscles. The spinal
cord may also manage reflex actions like escape or withdraw
responses, and house central pattern generators managing
rhythmic motor activity. Many different types of cell are
needed to perform these functions, including various inter-
neurons. While there are some important differences in spi-
nal cords between vertebrate groups, for example, in motor
control circuits in limbed vs finned vertebrates, cell types
and their distributions appear fundamentally conserved.12
The developmental origins of these different cell types
have been intensively studied in model systems, especially
in chick and mouse. Across the dorsal-ventral (DV) axis the
embryonic spinal cord is divided into multiple discrete
zones, each giving rise to a defined suite of cell types. Prolif-
erative progenitor cells are sited ventricularly (by the lumen
of the spinal cord) in stem cell zones that produce new cells
through an extended period of development. Cells born from
the same progenitor cell zone at different developmental times
may have different fates, leading to mediolateral (ML) diversity
of cell types at the same DV level. The spinal cord is also pat-
terned along the anterior-posterior (AP) axis. Before considering
the evolution of these axes, we will first summarise the molecu-
lar control of their development as understood from model
vertebrates.
3
| PATTERNING THE
VERTEBRATE SPINAL CORD: THE
AP AXIS
The spinal cord grows from anterior to posterior, in common
with other axial tissues such as the somitic and intermediate
FIGURE 1
Chordate phylogeny and developing spinal cord
anatomy. Amphioxus is a member of the earliest diverging chordate
lineage, and has a relatively simple spinal cord region that is just one
cell thick. Tunicates, such as the sea squirt Ciona, have highly reduced
spinal cord regions with just four cells in cross section and no neurons.
Vertebrates have much more complex spinal cords, with more cells
across the dorsal-ventral axis, and multiple cell layers across the
medial-lateral axis. Lamprey and jawed vertebrate spinal cord diagrams
are schematic, and differences are discussed more below. Diagrams
adapted from Reference 11
LEUNG AND SHIMELD
1029

mesoderm. New cells are born in the posterior growth zone,
the tailbud, and as these cells continue to proliferate the
tailbud grows posteriorly, leaving some cells behind in the
forming spinal cord.13 As developmental time passes, these
cells become progressively distant from the tailbud and may
start to differentiate. AP identity in spinal cord cells is regu-
lated by Hox gene expression. Establishment and mainte-
nance of Hox gene expression in the spinal cord is a complex
affair, involving regulation by multiple external signals, by
interactions between Hox genes, and by change in chromatin
state through developmental time. There is insufficient space
here to detail all of these, and the reader is referred to a recent
review for a detailed exploration of these issues.14 However,
some key generalizations can be extracted (Figure 2).
Wnt signaling plays a role in posterior specification in many
bilaterians,57 and in vertebrates is involved in early regulation of
the Hox complexes.14 It has also long been known from cell cul-
ture, explant and signaling pathway manipulation studies that
Hox genes differ in their sensitivity to signaling molecules like
retinoic acid (RA) and fibroblast growth factor (FGF). RA is
secreted from newly formed somites, while FGF (and other sig-
naling molecules like the Tgfβ family member Gdf11) are pro-
duced by the tailbud.58,59 Since both tailbud and somitogenesis
progress synchronously from anterior to posterior, all spinal cord
cells should in principle move through developmental time from
a high FGF, to a high RA environment. Exactly when and how
these signals regulate Hox expression though has been tricky to
decipher. Under RA treatment in chick embryos, the anterior
boundaries of neural tube expression Hoxb1, Hoxb3, Hoxb4,
and Hoxb5 extend, but their expression is not affected by FGF
treatment; conversely, FGF treatment anteriorises the expression
of Hoxb6, Hoxb7, Hoxb8, and Hoxb9, but these are not sensitive
to RA in the same assay.60 However, others showed that Hoxc6
and Hoxc9 expression in explants respond to both RA and FGF
treatment, with Hoxc6 expression increasing when treated with
RA and reducing when treated with FGF, whereas Hoxc9
expression responded reciprocally.59
Such differences in observation could be explained by stud-
ies using different methods and systems (eg, whole embryo
in vivo vs explants in vitro), but developmental time also plays
a critical role. Tailbud derived FGF regulates proliferation,58,61
but cells that spend more time in the tailbud appear to show
progressive opening of chromatin across Hox clusters.62 How
